items.misconception: 0.6 is larger because it has more digits.
items.random_wrong: 3/5 is larger because fractions are always smaller-looking but bigger.
items.why: Students don't fluently convert between fractions and decimals and compare them by surface features rather than value.

items.misconception: 2/5 — by adding numerators and denominators separately.
items.why: Students treat fraction addition as elementwise, applying the rule for fraction multiplication (which does multiply numerators and denominators) to addition.

items.q: How does the graph of y = f(x - 3) compare to y = f(x)?
items.correct: It is translated 3 units to the right.
items.misconception: It is translated 3 units to the left because of the minus.
items.random_wrong: It is reflected in the y-axis because of the minus.
items.why: Students apply the sign literally; the minus 'pushes left' intuitively, but x - 3 actually requires x to be larger to get the same output, shifting the graph right.

items.q: Which proof technique is best for disproving 'all primes are odd'?
items.why: It's counterexample. “induction” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Induction is most suited for statements about:
items.why: It's positive integers. “real numbers in general” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Smallest even prime (used as counterexample to 'all primes are odd').
items.why: Exact answer: 2. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
